content.definition: SVT is tachycardia originating above the ventricles, usually a regular narrow-complex rhythm. Mechanisms include AVNRT, AVRT, atrial tachycardia and atrial flutter with rapid ventricular response.

content.text: SVT matters after cardiac surgery because rates of 150-250/min drastically shorten diastolic filling, increase oxygen demand and reduce coronary perfusion. A post-valve, low-EF, RV failure or post-CABG patient may become unstable quickly.

items: Assess stability first: MAP, arterial waveform, chest pain, pulmonary oedema, mentation, lactate and urine output.
Confirm regularity and QRS width; wide-complex tachycardia in a cardiac patient is VT until proven otherwise.
If stable and regular narrow-complex, consider vagal manoeuvres and adenosine if appropriate.
Correct triggers such as hypoxia, electrolytes, ischaemia, catecholamines, pain, fever and atrial stretch.

content.text: Do not give adenosine to irregular wide-complex tachycardia or suspected pre-excited AF.
Recognition

SVT usually has QRS below 120 ms because ventricular activation uses the His-Purkinje system. It is often regular, abrupt in onset and offset, and commonly 150-250/min. P waves may be hidden, retrograde or abnormal.
AVNRT and AVRT
AVNRT uses a re-entry circuit around the AV node. AVRT uses an accessory pathway, as in WPW. Orthodromic AVRT is narrow; antidromic AVRT is wide and may mimic VT.
Atrial tachycardia and flutter

Atrial tachycardia arises from an ectopic atrial focus. Atrial flutter is macro-reentry, often with atrial rate around 300/min. Flutter with 2:1 conduction produces ventricular rate around 150/min and is commonly mistaken for SVT.
SVT versus sinus tachycardia
Haemodynamic consequences
At 180/min each cardiac cycle lasts about 0.33 seconds, leaving little time for ventricular filling or coronary perfusion. Stroke volume falls, oxygen demand rises and ischaemia may develop.
Vagal manoeuvres and adenosine

Vagal manoeuvres increase parasympathetic tone and may terminate AV-node-dependent SVT. Adenosine transiently blocks AV nodal conduction, terminating AVNRT/AVRT or revealing flutter waves. Standard adult dosing is 6 mg rapid IV push with flush, then 12 mg if required.
When adenosine is inappropriate

Avoid adenosine in irregular wide-complex tachycardia, suspected pre-excited AF, severe active bronchospasm, second- or third-degree AV block without pacing, sick sinus without pacing, or when VT is likely. It must not delay cardioversion in instability.
Synchronised cardioversion
Unstable SVT causing hypotension, shock, ischaemic discomfort, acute heart failure or altered mentation requires synchronised cardioversion. Confirm sync markers on QRS complexes before shock.
A regular narrow tachycardia at 150/min is flutter with 2:1 conduction until you have actively looked for flutter waves.
Adenosine is diagnostic as well as therapeutic because it can unmask flutter.
SVT in CTICU may be a sign of hypoxia, atrial stretch, inotrope excess or ischaemia.
Do not spend time on repeated vagal manoeuvres while the arterial waveform collapses.
ECG and monitor setup

For supraventricular tachycardia, the ECG monitor must be treated as a clinical device, not wallpaper. Use a lead that shows atrial activity when P waves matter, print strips during events, check electrode contact, and repeat a 12-lead when rhythm diagnosis, ischaemia or conduction disease is uncertain.
Arterial waveform correlation

The arterial line determines whether electrical activity is producing mechanical output. Rate, rhythm and monitor labels are secondary to MAP, pulse pressure, waveform amplitude, lactate, urine output and clinical perfusion.
Defibrillator and pacing readiness

Unstable SVT requires synchronised cardioversion; stable regular narrow SVT may need adenosine setup requires early access to pads, synchronised cardioversion, defibrillation or pacing depending on stability. In post-cardiac surgery patients, also check epicardial wires, pacing box battery, output, sensitivity, capture and sensing.

rows: Lead quality, rhythm strip, alarm limits and artefact.
Avoids missed rhythm change or false diagnosis.
Conduction, QRS width, ischaemia and baseline comparison.
Clarifies rhythm and identifies dangerous mimics.
Level, zero, damping, pulse pressure and waveform presence.
Pads, sync markers, battery and energy pathway.
Mode, rate, output, sensitivity and capture threshold.
Essential when rate or AV synchrony fails.
Suggests supraventricular activation if no aberrancy.
High risk for poor filling in CTICU.
6 mg rapid IV push with flush
Use proximal IV and ECG recording.
Hypotension, shock, ischaemia, acute heart failure or altered mentation

content.text: Escalate persistent SVT above 150/min, failed adenosine, symptoms or uncertain diagnosis.
Escalate urgently for SVT with hypotension, ischaemia, pulmonary oedema, low output, wide QRS or recurrence.
Activate emergency response for loss of pulse, peri-arrest SVT, severe shock or deterioration into VT/VF.

quiz.question: A regular narrow tachycardia around 150/min may be what rhythm?
quiz.explanation: Flutter often conducts 2:1 and gives a ventricular rate near 150/min.

quiz.question: What is the initial adult adenosine dose for appropriate stable regular SVT?
options: 6 mg rapid IV push with flush.
quiz.explanation: Adenosine must be delivered rapidly because its half-life is very short.

quiz.question: When should SVT be cardioverted?
options: When it causes hypotension, shock, ischaemia, acute heart failure or altered mentation.
